professor.name: Dr. Okafor
professor.text: We've been discussing how social media reshapes the way people form opinions on public issues. One pattern stands out: algorithms tend to show us content that confirms what we already believe, which can deepen divisions in society. Some sociologists argue that platforms have a responsibility to expose users to opposing viewpoints, while others believe that doing so would amount to manipulating what people see and undermine individual choice. Should social media companies be required to diversify the perspectives they show users? Why or why not?

students.name: Lena
students.text: I think companies should be required to do this. When people only encounter one perspective, they stop questioning their assumptions, and that fragments society into hostile camps. A modest requirement to surface credible opposing views wouldn't dictate beliefs; it would simply restore the kind of exposure that healthy public debate fundamentally depends on.

students.name: Marco
students.text: I'm skeptical of mandating this. Deciding which perspectives count as legitimate is itself a value judgment, and handing that power to corporations or regulators seems dangerous. People are capable of seeking out other views deliberately, and a forced feed of opposing content might just breed resentment rather than genuine understanding.

professor.name: Dr. Reyes
professor.text: Recently, a growing number of companies have adopted four-day workweeks without cutting employee pay, reporting that productivity stayed level or even rose. Supporters argue that concentrated, well-rested workers accomplish just as much in less time, while critics contend that the model only works for certain industries and could disadvantage businesses that rely on continuous customer service. If you were advising a mid-sized company, would you recommend trying a four-day workweek? Explain your reasoning.

students.name: Aisha
students.text: I'd recommend it cautiously. The productivity gains reported by early adopters suggest that long hours often mask inefficiency rather than reflect real output. A trial period would let the company measure consequences directly, and the boost to recruitment and retention alone could justify the experiment in a competitive labor market.

students.name: Ross
students.text: I wouldn't recommend it broadly. For firms whose value comes from being available to clients five or six days a week, compressing the schedule fundamentally changes the service they offer. The headline successes tend to come from knowledge-based companies, and generalizing from those cases strikes me as misleading.

professor.name: Dr. Gupta
professor.text: We've been examining how schools handle students who finish coursework far ahead of their peers. One increasingly common response is to let advanced students skip a grade entirely. Some educators argue that acceleration keeps gifted students challenged and engaged, while others worry that it places them socially among older classmates and can harm their emotional development. Do you think schools should allow academically advanced students to skip grades? Why or why not?

students.name: Jonah
students.text: I support letting them skip. Forcing a capable student to sit through material they've already mastered breeds boredom and even resentment toward school. The social concerns are real but often overstated; many accelerated students adjust well, and the intellectual stimulation outweighs a temporary adjustment period.

students.name: Phoebe
students.text: I'm more hesitant. Academic readiness and emotional maturity don't always develop at the same pace, and a twelve-year-old among sixteen-year-olds may struggle in ways grades can't capture. Enrichment within their own grade often challenges advanced students without the social disruption that skipping inevitably introduces.

professor.name: Dr. Lin
professor.text: We've been examining how cities try to reduce the heat that builds up in dense neighborhoods during summer. One proposal gaining traction is to require that all new buildings install reflective or planted ('green') roofs. Some scientists argue this is a cost-effective way to cool entire districts and cut energy use, while others contend that the upfront expense burdens developers and that the cooling effect is too localized to matter citywide. Should cities mandate green or reflective roofs on new construction? Why or why not?

students.name: Carlos
students.text: I think a mandate makes sense. The cumulative effect matters even if any single roof seems minor; widespread adoption could measurably lower neighborhood temperatures and reduce strain on the power grid during heat waves. The added construction cost is modest compared to the long-term public health and energy benefits.

students.name: Zoe
students.text: I'd be cautious about mandating it. Reflective roofs and vegetation perform very differently depending on climate and building type, so a blanket rule could force ineffective solutions in some cases. Offering incentives rather than requirements would let developers choose approaches genuinely suited to each project.

professor.name: Dr. Novak
professor.text: We've been discussing how people respond to receiving frequent feedback on their performance at work. Some workplaces now use apps that send employees continuous ratings and reminders throughout the day. Some psychologists argue that this steady stream of feedback accelerates learning and keeps people motivated, while others believe that constant evaluation heightens anxiety and erodes intrinsic interest in the work itself. Do you think continuous performance feedback helps or harms employees? Explain your view.

students.name: Amber
students.text: I lean toward it being helpful, within limits. Timely feedback lets people correct mistakes before they harden into habits, and ambiguity about how one is doing can be more stressful than clear information. The key is whether the feedback is constructive rather than purely a stream of numerical scores.

students.name: Eric
students.text: I think the harms tend to dominate. When evaluation never stops, people start working to optimize the metric rather than do meaningful work, and that undermines the genuine curiosity that drives the best performance. Occasional, thoughtful feedback respects autonomy in a way that relentless monitoring simply cannot.

professor.name: Dr. Hassan
professor.text: We've been examining the ethics of how streaming platforms decide what to recommend to viewers. These systems are typically optimized to maximize watch time, which critics say can trap users in hours of passive viewing. Some argue that platforms should instead optimize for viewer well-being, perhaps by nudging people toward stopping points, while others insist that a company's job is simply to give users more of what they enjoy. Should streaming services be designed to limit excessive viewing? Why or why not?

students.name: Yuki
students.text: I think they should. A system engineered purely to maximize watch time isn't neutral; it actively exploits psychological tendencies people struggle to resist. Building in gentle stopping cues would respect users as people with broader goals, not just as attention to be harvested for advertising revenue.

students.name: Will
students.text: I disagree that platforms should police this. Adults can decide for themselves how much to watch, and a company deliberately steering people away from its own product feels paternalistic. If excessive viewing is a problem, the remedy lies in personal habits and tools users opt into, not corporate design choices imposed on everyone.

professor.name: Dr. Diaz
professor.text: We've been discussing how governments can discourage consumption of sugary drinks, which are linked to rising rates of diabetes and obesity. Several cities have introduced taxes that raise the price of soda and other sweetened beverages. Supporters argue that higher prices meaningfully reduce consumption and fund health programs, while opponents counter that such taxes fall hardest on low-income families and rarely change deeply ingrained habits. Do you think governments should tax sugary drinks? Why or why not?

students.name: Hailey
students.text: I support these taxes. Evidence from cities that adopted them shows consumption genuinely declining, and the revenue can fund nutrition education in the communities most affected. A modest price signal nudges healthier choices without banning anything, which strikes me as a reasonable role for public policy.

students.name: Andy
students.text: I'm not convinced taxes are the right tool. They disproportionately burden poorer households who spend a larger share of income on groceries, yet wealthier consumers barely notice the difference. Addressing the root causes through education and better access to affordable fresh food would tackle the problem more equitably.

professor.name: Dr. Achebe
professor.text: We've been discussing proposals for a universal basic income—a fixed payment given to every citizen regardless of whether they work. Advocates argue it would cushion people against job losses from automation and reduce poverty without the stigma of traditional welfare, while skeptics worry it would discourage work and prove impossibly expensive to sustain. If your country were considering a universal basic income, would you support it? Explain your reasoning.

students.name: Diane
students.text: I'd cautiously support it. As automation displaces routine jobs, a stable income floor could give people the security to retrain or pursue work that machines can't replace. Pilot programs haven't shown the dramatic drop in employment that critics predict, which suggests the fear of mass idleness is overstated.

students.name: Mark
students.text: I remain skeptical. The cost of paying everyone, including those who don't need it, is staggering and would likely require tax increases or cuts elsewhere that hurt the vulnerable. Targeted support directed at those genuinely in need seems both more affordable and more effective than spreading money universally.
